speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is April 19th, 1996. The survivors of the Shoa is Itka Zygmuntowicz, Itka Frajman. The interviewer is Robert Czajkowski. The interview is taking place in Ciechanow in Poland. The language of the interview is English. Hello, I'm Robert Czajkowski-- C-Z-A-J-K-O-W-S-K-I. Today is the 19th, April, 19th, 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello, I'm Robert Czajkowski-- C-Z-A-J-K-O-W-S-K-I. Today is April 19th, 1996. I'm interviewing Itka Zygmuntowicz. The interview is taking place in Ciechanow in Poland. Itka, would you like to tell your name, date of birth, age, and city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I was born to religious Jewish parents-- Simo and Eljo Frajman, in a small town Ciechanow of Poland, located about 90 kilometers north of Warsaw, during Passover, April 15th, 1926. I was the oldest of three children. I had   a younger brother Srulek, a little sister Zeisel, and my maternal widowed grandmother, Chaye Sure Zebrah lived with us and helped to raise me. My first home was in Ciechanow, Poland, [POLISH] not far from where the soldiers used to be stationed. The reason I remember so exactly this is because I remember when I was a little girl, my father of blessed memory used to say, you know, Itkalo, the whole army that is stationed here can never hurt you as much as you can hurt yourself, when you don't behave morally and ethically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Itkala, how do feel being in Ciechanow after 35 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have extremely mixed emotions. I have wonderful memories till the age of 13. I have horrible memories from the age of 13 to 19. Because till the age of 13 was my world before Nazi destruction. From age 13 till 19 was my world during Nazi destruction and afterwards. And my oldest memory is of my home, because we lived in two homes. This was the first one. We didn't own this home, we rented it. And it was not far from the stations, where the soldiers were stationed. And it was-- I remember it exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entrance where we used to go was from the back. And in the back, there was a huge, huge field. We used to go out on one flight of steps. And we used to live there with the members of my family that I already  mentioned. And I used to play here. One of my closest friends of non-Jewish friends was a girl named Halinka. We used to have wonderful times. She would come to our house, and my mother always treated her. And when I came to her house, her mother tried to treat me with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, I would not eat all the food in the house, because we kept a kosher home. And Halinka once cried and said, Itkalo, that's not fair. I eat in your home. Why don't you eat in mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came and said that to my mother. And my mother said, Itkalo, we are Jewish. And we have to respect Halinka's holidays and customs. But we cannot eat everything. You can have fruit there or vegetables, but you cannot have meat in there. And Halinka and I were very good friends. We used to play together. And I remember one special incident when we were little. We use to tell each other how we celebrate holidays-- how we celebrate. And my mother once explained to me-- she said, you can share your holidays. You can visit to each other. But just like you cannot celebrate her birthday, because she's born on a different day, so she cannot celebrate yours. But you can share. She wanted to point out that I should tolerate her religion and her people and respect them. But at the same token, I should observe my own. Because this is who I am and who I represent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could I describe my room?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in every little detail. But I remember in the kitchen we had-- there was a square in the kitchen. And it had an iron ring. You pulled it up to go down to a dark root cellar. The reason why they called it the root cellar because we didn't have electricity in those days, so we didn't have refrigeration and all the modern appliances. So we would keep our food-- the perishable food to keep cold-- in the root cellar. I remember that when I looked through the window, I used to see many times the soldiers marching. And I played with children, because here I didn't live till age 13. I just lived the earliest years here. But I remember there was an attic. We used to go up to the attic as children, to play there. And what I remember is in the back of the house, there was a huge field. And it was so open. And I felt so free. And I used, like a child-- I used to look like this and pretend I'm a bird and run and play. In the winter, I remember when it was the first snow, I would run out and lay down in the snow, to leave a mark. And as a child, played all kinds of different kinds of games with children. And also the oldest memories I remember-- the holidays with my family. I remember the interaction with my family. So those were basically my childhood memories. Because at that time, I didn't go yet to school when I lived here. Maybe later on, I don't remember the exact date when we moved from here. But this was the place where I was born, the home where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was born in [POLISH], your father in Pultusk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything about the Jewish life in those places?

speaker: interviewee
text: From what I knew, my father used to tell me that his parents were also born in Pultusk. And they lived there all their lives. I wasn't fortunate to know either my grandfather, my father's parents, because they were no longer alive. My grandfather died when my father was a young boy. And my grandmother Zeisel, my grandfather [INAUDIBLE] died when my father was a young boy. And my grandmother Zeisel remarried. And she had two children from her second marriage-- [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]. And my father had one natural sister from the same parents. Her name was [? Sura. ?] And when my grandparents no longer were alive, my Aunt [? Sura ?] was married. And she took in the two orphaned girls. And she raised them, because we were a very close family. And as a child, I remember they came to visit Ciechanow. And my father and mother used to take me to visit there after. Actually, my parents met in Pultusk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had two occupations during the 13 years of my life. At the earliest time, he had a wholesale dairy. They used to buy milk, make butter, and all the things. And he had that with a partner. Obviously, it wasn't satisfactory for my father. So he later switched, and he made a living in photography and in picture framing. And he was the sole breadwinner in our family. My mother, before-- she met father in Pultusk, Poland. Because before she was married, she played in the Yiddish theater with the amateurs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where it was? Here in Ciechanow? Or in Pultusk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, maybe in Ciechanow too. But I know that I heard her telling that they met in Pultusk-- my father and mother, because that was the birthplace of my father-- when she came to play with the drama group, where you have the pictures. And that's where they met, they fell in love, and they got married. How and when they moved to Ciechanow, I have no idea. But  I know that my father's entire family lived in Pultusk when Germany invaded Poland. And my mother's family-- my   mother was one of seven siblings. Unfortunately, two died before I was born. So the others were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandmother had-- when Germany invaded Poland, she had a lot of children. In fact, she died-- my maternal widowed grandmother, Chaye Sure Zebrah, died shortly after Germany invaded Poland. They came September 1st, 1939. At the time of her death, she had five married children, 21 grandchildren, and one great grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one cousin of mine, who also was born in Poland by the same name, Gitel-- you know, her Hebrew name was Gitel, named after the same great grandmother-- she emigrated when I was 8 years old, 1934, to Palestine, to marry a boy there that she met in Ciechanow, Poland. His name was Chaim [? Shigoda. ?] And he left earlier with the first Maccabi. And then she left in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my grandmother-- only this cousin and one son who emigrated before I was even born were the only survivors of my entire family, of the people that I just mentioned. And this is just like the closest family. I'm not counting second cousins and relatives. Itakala, do you remember how many Jews were in Ciechanow before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe about 15,000. And  a little less than half of that was  Jewish. And it was a very close Jewish community. According to my memory, there was only one synagogue. But it had also Jewish studies near there. And people would come together there. And this was the place to meet in place. And the place was very connected. The home and the synagogue was very connected for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where the schools were you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to five, according to what I remember and what I've been also told. Because I met my kindergarten teacher when I came to the world gathering in 1981, Malka Fouks. According to her, my family brought me when I was five years old to her kindergarten. So I went there, I went to Hebrew school, of course. And I went to public school, till the age of 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many students were in your class in a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't remember. I don't remember exactly. I remember where the school was. The school was in the building of a man named Kalfus. He owned the building. And I have notes exactly where it is. I hope it's still there. I don't know, because, unfortunately, the first home where I was born and lived for a period of time, as we see now, is no longer in existence. And it was here, in this part where I'm standing. But the house was a little more to the front. The back of the house was here and then the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: [POLISH] Could you say something about the outhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I lived in Poland, we didn't have running water or electricity in our house, so we had an outhouse. And I remember when I was a little girl in the wintertime, it was so unpleasant, you had to go in the middle of the night to the outhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it the same outhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks to me. But since everything is torn down, so I don't know whether this was the one that we used, or it went down and this is the second one. This  I cannot say for sure. But it looks like the one that was when we lived here. And another thing I remember is behind the house is a huge field. And I used to love, as I mentioned before, used to love to play  in it. When I looked out, it looked so open and so free. Real-- like long, long, I remember it took me a long time to go through the whole piece of a field.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the apartments were not luxurious. But the open space here and the atmosphere. But most of all, why I loved it was because I had a loving family, who loved me unconditionally. So those are the things I missed. Thank you. What about this field?

speaker: interviewee
text: This field holds many pleasant memories. This was the back of our house, and it was huge. And I used to play here with the children. And most of all, what I like I remember, I used to pretend to-- play like a little bird, and run like this here, and feel so free, and play here. And it was wonderful memories. I loved this field, because it was so open and had a lot of fun in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any story which happened here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Connected especially with this here? Not more than I had good times here with the children. I've played with the children, all kinds of games and we played here. In the winter, when I came up, as I mentioned, I don't want to be repetitious. I loved when it was all full, because winters were cold in Poland. And there was a lot of snow. So I used to go out early from the house and stretch out-- go out and lay down, face up, and leave a mark. I don't remember how this was called in Polish. I no longer remember. But we used to play with children. We had wonderful times here. This is where my earliest years were spent, it was good years, happy years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here is the area of the old Jewish cemetery. Do you have anybody here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When my grandmother, Chaye Sure, my maternal grandmother, was buried here, shortly after Germany invaded Poland. And I'm sure also the cousin that I was telling you about, [? Handel, ?] was buried here in the old cemetery in 1931. And today, there is not a grave, not a marker, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's only the monument.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what I am grieved to see is the slogans of desecration on this memorial. Because there are no Jews. But the anti-Jewish feelings obviously didn't die with everybody. I met here a woman who was extremely kind and wonderful and helpful. On the other hand, I come here, and I see this here. After surviving Auschwitz, Birkenau, and other death marches and other concentration camps, this is breaking my heart, really. Because I thought that people learned something.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the other side is the graffiti. And they made slogans, anti-Jewish slogans, and also a swastika. Didn't they realize that the Nazis held the Polish people too? 6 million Jews have perished. They will never become what they had the potential of becoming. The world will never know how many wonderful people we have lost, and how many Nazi murderers we have gained. So what an exchange have they made? What an exchange? And this didn't teach people enough? This didn't the teach that we all have a right to live, that we're all born from the same God, we share the same Universe? This is very upsetting, very painful, very halting me  a lot, this slogan today. After this here, I don't regret I came here. Because this is where I was born, and I remember wonderful times here until the age of 13. But coming here now, hoping to revive some memories and finding here-- has really saddened me. That's the least, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, this used to be the old cemetery. Even before Germany came in, there was already a  new cemetery, which was much farther from here. And here in the back there were houses. My girlfriend used to live here and other people. And it was a very populated, Jewish community and now this happened-- no graves, no tombstones, no markers. You vanished all in agony, smoke, and flames, bereft and broken-hearted. On my soul, I engraved their names. I can't forget you, dear sisters and brothers. I remember your anguish and cry. Six million innocent men, women, and little children, oh God, who did not want to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very disturbed. Because as I arrived here, I found a woman who lives in Poland and was extremely kind to me, even offered to show me around. And this-- in contrast to this kind woman that I just met, never knew, and shows a lot of kindness to me-- I'm coming to the place where my grandmother and other relatives have been buried at this cemetery. And I found that even though there are no Jews, I still see a swastika. And there it says, 'Jude raus.' And this was what the Nazis said to me, when they came in the middle of the night to chase us out from Ciechanow, Poland on October 15th, 1941, and put us to the ghetto. And here I see a slogan, which I heard before the Nazis chased me out. And the slogan said in Polish, [POLISH], which means the houses are yours, but the streets are ours. Now, and here it says, [POLISH], Poland is yours. I didn't come here to take away Poland. I came here to remember the wonderful times I had here as a child. And frankly, when I see this here, they can keep Poland. They can keep Poland. I didn't come to take away anything, only to revive memories and to share them. Because I am a witness to the darkest chapter of human history. If people can still do this, and other people didn't even bother to erase it or to do something, I'm deeply disappointed and hurt. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here is [POLISH]. Do you remember this square?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is the square. It used to be called the market, in Yiddish, the [YIDDISH]. And here was the most contact that the Jewish people had with the Polish people. Because the Jewish people-- like shoemakers, and tailors, and other craftsmen-- would bring their merchandise, what they made by hand. And the people from the small villages around would bring livestock and would bring things that  were growing-- vegetables and fruit. And it used to be so busy, twice a week, and so colorful. And I remember that we used to come here-- and just to point out the changes, now, when we are in America, we always look to eat food that is not fat. In Polish, when we used to buy from the peasants a chicken, we used to blow on the back of the chicken to see if it's yellow, if it's a lot of fat.

words.text: if it's a lot of fat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: To take the relationships between non-Jewish people-- Polish-- and the Jewish people that had most of the relationships here. And they used to exchange what they brought to sell and what they brought able to sell. And it was  a warm friendship in this place. And people came here. And usually the Jewish people came her to buy for the Sabbat. They bought all the things. And not only the food was different. But I remember something now, when I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop, stop. Here is the [POLISH] Street. Do you remember this square?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember this square, because I used to come here sometimes twice a week. Because twice a week, they had the market here, where the peasants from the small villages would bring livestock, vegetables, and fruit that they grew to sell. On the other hand, the Jewish people who made-- shoemakers brought their shoes, their boots, tailors bought their clothes, and other merchants whatever they brought, and they used to exchange.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember something. I used to always get a kick out of when sometimes they would barter, he said, I'll pay you. He said, how much do you want? He would say so many zloty. And he would say, I give you so much. And may I see you for a moment please? And they would deal like this. OK, I give you 5 zloty. He said, I give you 4 zloty. And then he said, OK, let's make it 3 zloty, shake. And they shook.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- I remember as a child seeing this here. And what I also remember, the difference in our perception of what good food is. When we used to buy livestock-- chickens, ducks, geese, or turkeys-- they used to blow at the back of the chicken, to see if it's fat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fatter it was, the better it was. And I myself was very thin when I was young. And some people would ask my mother, does she have consumption? Because women at that time it was modern to be more, how do you say it? A little-- Thinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plump. More plump was modern. That used to be a status symbol of being-- we didn't have like we have now all the modern conveniences. So what we used to do when we brought home whatever we bought, a chicken or a turkey, we would go to the Jewish ritual slaughterer. And he would slaughter it. We would take it home. And then when we plucked it ourselves, we couldn't go like we can in the United States and buy ready-made-- go into the butcher store and get chickens and turkeys that are clean. And what I remember is that whenever my parents or grandmother would buy a duck or a goose, the down of it-- they would pluck it and put it separate. And they used to say, this is going to be for a trousseau to make feather beds, quilts, and pillows from down. And this is supposed to be for the trousseau. But unfortunately, none of them lived to see me married and to see me grow up. And the marketplace was teeming with people always. And the Jewish people mainly came here. The most was before the Sabbat. Everybody would prepare. Most of the people in town kept the Jewish customs and traditions-- had kosher homes. And they would come and buy here the food and start preparing. And we didn't have-- as I mentioned before-- electricity or running water in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to cook everything on our cast-iron stove. And in the winter we had a tile oven, to heat the house. And I regret that we didn't take a photograph of the oven that I took a picture of to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: But mostly what that market represented is the feeling, the business transaction that people have and the relationship they found. They were--
